coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Instance-Confidence Embedding (ICE), a method for handling instance-dependent label noise by using a per-instance scalar confidence parameter learned via embedding. This approach aims to approximate the instance-dependent transition matrix without the high computational cost of estimating a full KxK matrix for every sample.

The paper presents an intuitive and practical approach to a challenging problem in robust learning. It effectively bridges the gap between complex transition matrix modeling and simple label-smoothing techniques, offering a scalable solution that performs well on standard benchmarks.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Theoretical Identifiability: The proposed method relies on a scalar confidence parameter C to approximate the KxK transition matrix T(x). However, the paper does not provide conditions under which this scalar approximation is identifiable. Without such conditions, it is unclear if the learned confidence C truly reflects the underlying noise structure or if it is merely an artifact of the model's capacity to overfit to specific training labels. The authors should clarify the assumptions under which the scalar approximation remains a valid surrogate for the true instance-dependent transition matrix.

- Scalability and Memory Constraints for Large Datasets: The methodology assigns a trainable parameter to every instance in the training set (Section 3.3). While the authors argue this is acceptable for datasets like CIFAR-10, this becomes a significant bottleneck for large-scale datasets with millions of instances. The paper lacks a discussion on how this memory footprint scales or whether the embedding can be shared or compressed. A more rigorous analysis of the trade-off between the number of parameters and the dataset size is required to justify the claim of efficiency.

- Sensitivity to Initialization and Optimization: The instance-confidence embedding is initialized and optimized alongside the main classifier. Given that the confidence parameters are learned per-instance, the model could easily collapse into a trivial solution where it assigns low confidence to all difficult samples regardless of their true label. The paper does not analyze the sensitivity of the results to the initialization of these embeddings or the interaction between the learning rates of the classifier and the confidence parameters. Providing ablation studies on the initialization strategy or the optimization dynamics would strengthen the empirical claims.

- Assumption of Clean Validation for Model Selection: The experiments use standard benchmarks where the noise is synthetically generated, allowing for the use of clean test sets for evaluation. However, the paper claims the method is useful for real-world scenarios where clean labels are unavailable. There is no discussion on how to perform model selection or hyperparameter tuning in the absence of a clean validation set. The authors should address how the proposed method behaves in a setting where no clean data is available for monitoring performance.

- Absence of a formal identifiability example for the scalar approximation: The paper claims that a scalar confidence parameter C can approximate the KxK instance-dependent transition matrix T(x). However, it does not provide a worked example or a specific DGP where the true noise is instance-dependent and the scalar approximation successfully recovers the underlying clean class posterior. To demonstrate that this result has bite, the authors should construct a simple, fully-specified synthetic model with a known KxK transition matrix T(x) and show that the ICE method converges to the true clean posterior p(Y|X) while the class-conditional noise (CCN) baseline fails. This would clarify the conditions under which the scalar approximation is non-vacuous.

- Lack of guidance on hyperparameter selection without clean validation data: The paper positions the ICE method as a tool for real-world scenarios where clean labels are scarce, yet the empirical evaluation relies on synthetic noise where clean test sets are available for model selection. The paper lacks a practical implementation discussion on how to select the learning rate or the regularization strength for the instance embeddings when no clean validation set is available. The authors should propose and evaluate a heuristic, such as monitoring the distribution of the learned confidence parameters or using a small subset of cross-validation, to guide practitioners in real-world deployment.

- Missing comparison to existing methods in a low-data regime: The authors argue that instance embedding is acceptable because the number of instances is usually smaller than the parameters of the classifier. However, they do not compare the performance of ICE against simpler, non-parametric baselines (e.g., k-nearest neighbor-based noise filtering) in a low-data regime where the overhead of learning N instance-specific parameters might lead to significant overfitting. A simulation comparing ICE to a standard robust baseline on a small-scale dataset (e.g., 500-1000 samples) would demonstrate whether the instance-confidence embedding effectively captures noise or simply memorizes the noisy labels.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal analysis or set of conditions under which the scalar confidence approximation is identifiable.

- Include an empirical analysis of how the memory and computational requirements grow with dataset size, specifically addressing the scalability concerns of per-instance embeddings.

- Add an ablation study on the initialization of confidence embeddings and the impact of the learning rate schedule on the separation of noisy vs. clean instances.

- Discuss and demonstrate how the model performs or how hyperparameters can be selected in a scenario without a clean validation set.

comments.title: Missing Methodological Framework
comments.quote:
In this section, we present our proposed method, *instance-confidence embedding* (ICE).

###
comments.explanation: The paper lacks a formal definition of the ICE methodology. The text introduces the concept but fails to provide the mathematical formulation, the objective function, or the algorithmic steps required to implement the embedding. Please include the complete methodology, including the formal definition of the embedding and the derivation of the learning objective, to allow for a meaningful review.

comments.title: Unjustified Scalar Approximation
comments.quote: Concretely, noting the fact that most columns of the IDN transition matrix have only limited influence on the class-posterior estimation, we propose a variational approximation that uses a single-scalar confidence parameter.
comments.explanation: The claim that most columns of the IDN transition matrix have limited influence is asserted without justification. In a general KxK transition matrix T(x), the influence of a column depends entirely on the distribution of the true labels p(Y|x). If p(Y=k|x) is large, the k-th column of T(x) is dominant, not negligible. Please provide a formal condition or a reference supporting why a single scalar can approximate this matrix across diverse datasets; otherwise, the variational approximation remains an unmotivated heuristic.

comments.title: Argmax-Preserving Property Violation
comments.quote: ire that  $\arg \max(\pmb{p}) = \arg \max(\pmb{q})$ , i.e.,  $\pmb{q} = h(\pmb{p}; C)$  should be an argmax-preserving function so that the top-1 index of the probability vector does not cha
comments.explanation: The linear interpolation in Equation (8) fails the argmax-preserving requirement for C < 1. As C approaches 0, the vector q approaches the uniform distribution u, where the argmax is no longer well-defined in the same sense as p. Specifically, if p is not strictly greater than u, the linear combination can cause the relative order of components to collapse toward the uniform distribution. Please clarify that the argmax-preserving property is only strictly guaranteed for the power transformation (Equation 9) or under specific constraints on p and C.

comments.title: Inconsistent Terminology for Noisy Labels
comments.quote: We also demonstrate that there already exist ambiguous or unlabeled training examples in the original datasets which can be detected by the proposed method.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'unlabeled instances' in the context of a noisy label classification task. However, the problem setting concerns learning with noisy labels, where every training instance has a label, albeit a potentially incorrect one. Using the term 'unlabeled' is confusing and suggests a semi-supervised learning setting that is not formally defined. Please replace 'unlabeled' with 'mislabeled' or 'noisy' throughout the paper to maintain consistency.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Instance-Confidence Embedding (ICE) to approximate instance-dependent noise by assigning a trainable scalar confidence parameter to each training instance. The method shows competitive accuracy on semi-synthetic image and text datasets and claims utility for detecting mislabeled samples. However, the theoretical framing, experimental comparisons, and deployment strategy contain significant gaps that need addressing.

The paper tackles a relevant problem in noisy label learning by proposing a lightweight, instance-specific confidence parameterization. The empirical results demonstrate reasonable performance on standard benchmarks, and the visualization of confidence distributions provides intuitive insights into label ambiguity. The core idea of decoupling instance-level noise estimation from the classifier is promising for practical applications.

Overview issues:

- Mischaracterization of the optimization objective as variational inference: Section 3.1 frames the learning process as maximizing a variational lower bound (Eq. 4), yet the derivation introduces no latent variables or marginal likelihood. The KL divergence in Eq. 4 simply measures the discrepancy between the true noisy posterior and the model's parametric approximation. Maximizing Eq. 6 reduces to standard cross-entropy minimization on the observed noisy labels. This terminology obscures the actual mechanics of the method and misleads readers about its theoretical foundations. The authors should remove the variational framing and explicitly state that the method directly optimizes a parametric surrogate for the noisy label distribution. If a latent variable interpretation is intended, it must be formally defined and derived.

- Identifiability and overfitting risks with per-instance embeddings: Section 3.3 introduces a trainable scalar parameter for every training instance. While this flexibility allows the model to capture non-smooth noise patterns, it also creates a severe overfitting risk, particularly at the reported ~52% noise rate. The paper claims the embeddings successfully separate flipped from original labels, but provides no theoretical guarantees or empirical correlation analysis to prove that the learned confidences track true corruption rather than memorization. With sparse gradients, the optimizer could easily drive C to trivial values that fit the noise without improving generalization. The authors should add an ablation correlating learned C with ground-truth noise labels and test across lower noise rates (e.g., 20% and 40%) to demonstrate robustness.

- Ambiguous test-time inference protocol: The instance embedding is explicitly non-generalizable to unseen data, yet the paper does not clarify how the model handles inference on a test set. If the confidence parameter C is disabled or set to 1 during testing, the IDN modeling only influences training dynamics, effectively reducing the method to a custom loss reweighting scheme. This directly contradicts the stated motivation of using instance-specific noise information for downstream tasks like data cleansing or active learning, which require confidence scores for new examples. The authors must specify the test-time protocol and either propose a mechanism to generalize the confidence scores or reframe the contribution as a training-time regularization technique.

- Omission of direct competitors in the experimental comparison: The related work and literature context highlight Confidence-Scored IDN (CSIDN) as a highly relevant baseline that also leverages instance-level confidence to model IDN. Table 1 and Table 2 completely omit CSIDN and other recent transition matrix estimators like MEIDTM or PTD. Without these comparisons, it is impossible to determine whether the proposed instance embedding approach offers genuine improvements over existing confidence-based methods or merely replicates their performance with a different parameterization. The experimental section should include CSIDN and at least one other recent IDN method, along with a discussion of their relative strengths and weaknesses.

- Insufficient detail on noise generation and limited dataset scope: Section 5.1 mentions generating instance-dependent noise using a method similar to Zhang et al. (2021a) but lacks explicit details on the corruption mechanism. The reliance on semi-synthetic data with artificially high noise rates (~52%) limits the validity of claims regarding real-world applicability. Real-world IDN often follows complex, asymmetric patterns that a single scalar confidence parameter may struggle to approximate. The authors should provide a clear description of the noise generation process and include experiments on real-world noisy datasets to validate claims of real-world applicability.

- Downstream application experiments are completely absent: The abstract and introduction repeatedly promise that the learned confidence scores can drive downstream tasks like data cleansing, active learning, or learning with rejection. The experimental section stops at classification accuracy and qualitative inspection of low-confidence examples. Readers will wonder how well the confidence ranking actually isolates corrupted labels in practice. Without precision-recall curves or ablation studies that remove low-confidence samples, the claimed utility remains speculative. The authors should compute the detection accuracy of mislabeled instances using a threshold on C and demonstrate performance gains after filtering or reweighting the training set.

- No quantitative analysis of the scalar approximation error: The core theoretical motivation rests on the claim that a single scalar confidence parameter suffices because most columns of the transition matrix have limited influence when the true posterior is confident. The paper illustrates this geometrically in Fig. 2 but never quantifies the approximation error. A natural question is whether the scalar approximation breaks down for specific noise structures or when the classifier is initially uncertain. The authors should derive the L2 or KL divergence between the true noisy posterior and the ICE approximation for a simple 2-class or 3-class model. This computation would show exactly where the approximation holds and where it introduces bias.

- Scalability and optimization details for instance embeddings are missing: Assigning a trainable scalar to every training instance creates a parameter count that scales linearly with the dataset size. The paper acknowledges this cost but provides no practical guidance for scaling to modern datasets like ImageNet or large-scale text corpora. Readers will wonder how the embeddings are initialized, whether they require regularization to prevent trivial solutions, and how sparse gradient updates are managed during training. The authors should specify the initialization scheme, discuss any L2 or entropy regularization applied to the embeddings, and report training time or memory overhead relative to the baseline classifier.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper mischaracterizes its optimization objective as variational inference without latent variables, leaves the test-time inference protocol undefined despite claiming utility for downstream tasks, and omits critical baselines like CSIDN. Addressing these theoretical, methodological, and experimental gaps is necessary before the claims can be considered robust.

Revision targets:

- Remove the variational inference framing in Section 3.1 and explicitly define the optimization objective as direct cross-entropy minimization on a parametric surrogate, or formally introduce a latent variable model if variational methods are intended.

- Clarify the test-time inference protocol and either provide a mechanism to generalize confidence scores to unseen data or reframe the method as a training-time regularization technique.

- Include CSIDN and at least one other recent IDN baseline in the experimental comparison to establish the method's relative performance.

- Add an ablation study correlating learned confidence scores with ground-truth noise labels across varying noise rates to demonstrate identifiability and guard against memorization.

- Provide explicit details on the IDN generation process and include experiments on at least one real-world noisy dataset to validate claims of real-world applicability.

comments.title: Logical gap between sparsity and scalar approximation
comments.quote: noting the fact that most columns of the IDN transition matrix have only limited influence on the class-posterior estimation, we propose a variational approximation that uses a single-scalar confidence parameter.
comments.explanation: The premise does not justify the reduction. Observing that most columns of the transition matrix exert limited influence only tells us the matrix is sparse or diagonally dominant. It does not logically entail collapsing the entire noise distribution into a single scalar confidence value. A scalar parameter only models the probability that the observed label is correct. It silently assumes that the exact distribution over incorrect classes is either negligible or uniformly irrelevant. The derivation needs to explicitly state this assumption or show how the scalar approximation preserves the necessary information for posterior estimation. Rewrite the motivation to acknowledge that the scalar model ignores off-diagonal noise structure, or provide a mathematical justification for why that structure can be safely discarded.

comments.title: Contradiction between per-instance parameters and test-time detection
comments.quote: we suggest using instance embedding that assigns a trainable parameter to each instance. The resulting instance-confidence embedding (ICE) method not only performs well under label noise but also can effectively detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances.
comments.explanation: Assigning a free trainable parameter to every training instance creates a memorization mechanism rather than a generalizable confidence function. This architectural choice directly conflicts with the claim that the method can effectively detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances on unseen data or for downstream applications like data cleansing. The per-instance embeddings are strictly tied to the training set and cannot be evaluated on new examples without a separate generalization mechanism. The authors should clarify whether the detection capability is limited to the training distribution or describe how the confidence scores are projected onto the test set. Add a sentence specifying the inference protocol for the confidence embeddings.

comments.title: Mismatch between approximation target and variational bound
comments.quote: we use a simpler function $q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)$ parameterized by $\theta$ as a *variational approximation* to $p_{\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)$.
comments.explanation: The text states that $q_{\theta,\phi}$ is introduced as an approximation to the parametric estimate $p_{\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)$. However, Eq. (4) and the subsequent learning objective in Eq. (6) treat $q_{\theta,\phi}$ as an approximation to the true noisy posterior $p(\widetilde{Y}|X)$ to derive the lower bound. This creates a conceptual disconnect: if $q$ is meant to approximate $p_{\phi}$, the bound should be derived for $\mathbb{E}_{p_{\phi}}[\log p_{\phi}]$, not $\mathbb{E}_{p}[\log p]$. Conversely, if the goal is to bound the true expected log-likelihood, $q$ should be framed as approximating $p$. Rewrite the approximation description to state that $q_{\theta,\phi}$ approximates $p(\widetilde{Y}|X)$, and consistently substitute $p$ for $p_{\phi}$ in Eq. (4) because the variational target must match the distribution being bounded to maintain logical consistency throughout the derivation.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that argmax change prevents learning
comments.quote: Otherwise, if  $h$  is too flexible and is able to map a confident prediction to a different confident prediction, then the output of  $f$  could be arbitrary, and consequently, no information of the true label can be learned from the noisy label supervision.
comments.explanation: The assertion that allowing $h$ to change the argmax implies the model learns "no information of the true label" is mathematically and empirically unfounded. A transformation that alters the top-1 class does not render the underlying representation $f(X)$ arbitrary; it merely shifts the decision boundary. The model can still minimize cross-entropy or other losses with respect to the noisy labels, and the learned features may still capture signal from the true distribution. The restriction to argmax-preserving $h$ is a design choice for stability or interpretability, not a strict necessity for learnability. Rewrite the claim to frame the restriction as a stability preference rather than an unfounded assertion about learnability.

comments.title: Missing embedding parameter in transformation function
comments.quote: L (\theta , \phi) = \text{cross-entropy} \left(\overbrace{h\left(\underbrace{f(X;\phi)}_{\text{p. neural network classifier, Eq. (1)} C.\text{instance-confidence embedding}}\right)}^{\text{q. fixed transformation, Eq. (8) and (9)}}, \widetilde{Y}\right)
comments.explanation: The LaTeX command \underbrace{f(X;\phi)}_{\text{...} C.\text{...}} places the text "C. instance-confidence embedding" strictly inside the subscript label, not as a mathematical variable. As written, the argument passed to $h$ is exclusively $f(X;\phi)$. Because the section explicitly defines $C$ as a trainable instance-confidence embedding that must be combined with the classifier output to approximate the noisy posterior, $C$ must appear as an explicit argument to $h$. Rewrite the equation to explicitly show $C$ as an input to $h$, such as $h(f(X;\phi), C)$, and adjust the underbrace/overbrace labels to match the corrected mathematical expression.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in KL divergence argument
comments.quote: \mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{\widetilde{Y}\sim p(\widetilde{Y}|X)}[\log p(\widetilde{Y}|X)]=D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p_{\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)\parallel q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X))+\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;X)
comments.explanation: The left-hand side takes an expectation over the true noisy posterior $p(\widetilde{Y}|X)$, while the KL divergence term uses $p_{\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)$ as its first argument. By definition, $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \parallel Q) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}[\log P(x) - \log Q(x)]$. For the algebraic identity in Eq. (4) to hold, the distribution inside the expectation on the LHS must match the first argument of the KL divergence. Since $p_{\phi}$ is explicitly defined as a parametric estimate rather than the true distribution, mixing $p$ and $p_{\phi}$ breaks the derivation. Rewrite Eq. (4) as $\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{\widetilde{Y}\sim p(\widetilde{Y}|X)}[\log p(\widetilde{Y}|X)]=D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p(\widetilde{Y}|X)\parallel q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X))+\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;X)$ because the expectation domain must match the first argument of the KL divergence for the algebraic identity to hold.

comments.title: Incorrect citation year and mischaracterization of prior work
comments.quote: Several methods have been reported in the literature, but they either only focus on binary classification under strong assumptions *(Menon et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2020)*, are based on domain-specific knowledge *(Xia et al., 2020)*, or need extra supervision *(Berthon et al., 2021)*.
comments.explanation: The bibliography lists Cheng et al. as an ICLR 2021 paper, making the in-text citation of 2020 inconsistent with the reference list. This should be updated to 2021. Additionally, characterizing Berthon et al. (2021) as requiring "extra supervision" misrepresents the method. CSIDN estimates instance-level noise transitions using the model's own predicted confidence scores during training, without any external annotations. The authors should correct the year and revise the description of Berthon et al. to accurately reflect its reliance on model-generated confidence.

comments.title: Notation in Eq. (3) conflates true and model quantities
comments.quote: p _ {\phi} (\widetilde {Y} | X) := \operatorname {C a t e g o r i c a l} (\widetilde {Y} | \boldsymbol {q} = \sum_ {Y = 1} ^ {K} p (\widetilde {Y} | Y, X) p _ {\phi} (Y | X))
comments.explanation: Eq. (3) mixes ground-truth quantities ($p(\widetilde{Y} | Y, X)$) with model-approximated quantities ($p_\phi(\cdot)$) under a single notation prefix. The preceding text also claims the LHS is $p(\widetilde{Y} | X)$, creating a three-way notational conflict. While the authors likely intend $p_\phi$ to represent the model's current working estimate, combining it with the unparameterized true noise transition obscures whether the equation describes a theoretical identity or a practical approximation. Rewrite Eq. (3) as $p(\widetilde{Y} | X) = \sum_{Y=1}^K p(\widetilde{Y} | Y, X) p(Y | X)$ to define the true posterior, and reserve $p_\phi$ strictly for the model's estimate $\widehat{p}(\widetilde{Y} | X) = \sum_{Y=1}^K \widehat{T}(x) p_\phi(Y | X)$ because this separation prevents ambiguity between data-generating processes and learnable parameters.

comments.title: Explicit normalization in temperature scaling equivalence
comments.quote:
Concretely, if $\bm{p}_{i}\propto\exp\{f_{i}(X;\phi)\}$ for $i=1,\ldots,K$, then Eq. (9) becomes

$\bm{q}_{i}\propto\exp\{Cf_{i}(X;\phi)\},$ (10)

which shows that $C$ is the *reciprocal of the temperature*.
comments.explanation: The use of the proportionality symbol in Eq. (10) omits the softmax normalization denominator. While this is a common shorthand, it weakens the explicit connection to temperature scaling, which is formally defined as a normalized distribution over scaled logits. To make the equivalence rigorous and directly comparable to the cited TS literature, rewrite Eq. (10) as $\bm{q}_{i}=\frac{\exp\{Cf_{i}(X;\phi)\}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}\exp\{Cf_{j}(X;\phi)\}}$. This removes ambiguity about the distribution's support and clearly demonstrates that $C$ scales the logits before normalization, matching the standard TS formulation.

comments.title: Citation inconsistency between Chen and Cheng et al. (2021)
comments.quote: s perspectives. Examples include *data cleansing* *(Liu et al., 2008; Northcutt et al., 2019; Hara et al., 2019)* that first removes harmful instances and then (re-)trains the model on the remaining subset; *dynamic training sample selection* *(Malach and Shalev-Shwartz, 2017; Jiang et al., 2018; Han et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2019; Mirzasoleiman et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a)* that selects training examples dynamically
comments.explanation: The text cites both "Chen et al., 2021" and "Cheng et al., 2021" in the same parenthetical list. The provided reference list only includes a 2021 paper by Hao Cheng et al. ("Learning with instance-dependent label noise: A sample sieve approach"). It is highly likely that "Chen et al., 2021" is a typographical error for "Cheng et al., 2021". If a different paper by Chen is intended, it must be added to the references. Please verify the intended citation and correct the author name or add the missing reference to avoid confusion. Remove "Chen et al., 2021; " from the citation list because it duplicates the intended reference to Cheng et al. (2021) and creates an unnecessary cross-reference error.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding computational cost
comments.quote: However, unlike explicit sample selection methods, the resulting algorithm is lightweight and has a low computational cost.
comments.explanation: The proposed method assigns a trainable scalar parameter to every training instance. This means the parameter count scales linearly with the dataset size $N$. For modern datasets like ImageNet or large-scale text corpora, this introduces millions of additional parameters, which significantly increases memory overhead during training and storage. While the per-batch computational cost of evaluating these scalars is indeed low, describing the method as "lightweight" is misleading without clarifying that the cost refers to forward/backward pass time rather than total memory footprint. The authors should rephrase this claim to specify that the method is computationally efficient per iteration, while acknowledging the linear memory scaling with dataset size.

comments.title: Misuse of 'unlabeled' terminology
comments.quote: In this section, we experimentally verify if the proposed instance-confidence embedding (ICE) method is able to differentiate unlabeled instances from correct ones and consequently improve the classification performance. We also demonstrate that there already exist ambiguous or unlabeled training examples in the original datasets which can be detected by the proposed method.
comments.explanation: The term "unlabeled" appears twice in this paragraph, but the paper exclusively addresses learning with *noisy* or *mislabeled* data. The Abstract, Introduction, and Conclusion consistently use "noisy labels," "mislabeled instances," and "ambiguous instances." Using "unlabeled" incorrectly implies missing ground-truth labels rather than corrupted ones, which contradicts the problem setup and could confuse readers about the experimental protocol. Rewrite "unlabeled" as "mislabeled" or "noisy" in both instances to align with the manuscript's core terminology.

comments.title: Inaccurate reference to GLUE evaluation metrics
comments.quote: The performance in terms of the suggested evaluation metric was reported in Table 2.
comments.explanation: The GLUE benchmark comprises multiple tasks that require different evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy for CoLA, F1 for MRPC, Pearson correlation for STS-B). Referring to a singular "suggested evaluation metric" is inaccurate and misleading for a multi-task benchmark. Readers will assume a single metric applies to all results, which obscures how performance is actually measured. Rewrite "The performance in terms of the suggested evaluation metric was reported in Table 2." as "The performance, using the task-specific evaluation metrics recommended for each GLUE task, is reported in Table 2." because this accurately reflects the benchmark's multi-metric structure.

comments.title: Grammatical fragment starting with Although
comments.quote: Although, if the dataset is relatively clean, the improvement might be marginal.
comments.explanation: This sentence is a grammatical fragment. "Although" is a subordinating conjunction and cannot stand alone as a complete sentence. It leaves the reader waiting for a main clause that never arrives. Rewrite "Although, if the dataset is relatively clean, the improvement might be marginal." as "However, if the dataset is relatively clean, the improvement might be marginal." because "However" correctly functions as a conjunctive adverb to introduce a contrasting statement without creating a fragment.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about CoLA annotation errors
comments.quote: We found that several ungrammatical sentences were mislabeled as acceptable, and some syntactically acceptable sentences were labeled as unacceptable by annotators possibly because they have semantic errors.
comments.explanation: The CoLA dataset annotation guidelines explicitly instruct annotators to judge grammatical acceptability independent of semantic plausibility. Attributing labeling errors to annotators confusing semantics with grammar contradicts standard annotation protocols for this dataset. The claim requires evidence or a softer phrasing to avoid misrepresenting the dataset's creation process. Rewrite the sentence to acknowledge that the low confidence scores may reflect inherent ambiguity in human judgments or dataset noise, rather than a direct confusion of semantics and grammar by annotators.

comments.title: Reverse KL divergence optimization properties
comments.quote: Since the KL-divergence is always non-negative, this term gives a *variational lower bound* of the expected log-likelihood.
comments.explanation: The derivation relies on $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p \parallel q) \ge 0$, which corresponds to the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence. While mathematically valid for establishing the lower bound, minimizing $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p \parallel q)$ (or equivalently maximizing $\mathbb{E}_p[\log q]$) exhibits zero-forcing behavior and can lead to poor approximation quality when $q$ has lighter tails than $p$. Standard variational inference typically minimizes the forward divergence $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q \parallel p)$ to achieve mode-covering behavior. Add a methodological justification for selecting reverse KL divergence after Eq. (4) to address the gap in explaining how the zero-forcing property impacts learning under label noise and why it is preferred over the standard forward KL formulation.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of the smoothing target in Eq. (8)
comments.quote: In contrast, in our method, it is the prediction $\bm{p}$ that is “smoothed”, not the label.
comments.explanation: This claim appears to contradict the standard formulation of instance-confidence methods and likely misstates Eq. (8). Instance-dependent noise approaches that use a confidence parameter $C$ typically construct a smoothed target distribution $\tilde{y} = C y + (1-C) \frac{1}{K}$, which directly interpolates the hard label with a uniform distribution. If Eq. (8) defines such a target, then the method smooths the label, not the prediction $\bm{p}$. Smoothing the model's output $\bm{p}$ is non-standard and would require explicit justification, as it alters the gradient signal in a way that differs from label smoothing or bootstrapping. The authors should verify whether Eq. (8) operates on the target or the prediction and correct the text accordingly.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a variational approximation to instance-dependent label noise using a scalar confidence parameter per instance, implemented via instance embedding. The idea is intuitive and the method is simple, which are strengths. However, the paper has significant issues: the theoretical justification for the variational bound is flawed, the approximation's validity is unsubstantiated, the experimental evaluation is incomplete (no real-world noisy datasets, no ablation studies, no statistical rigor), and the claims about detecting mislabeled instances are overstated without proper validation. These problems undermine the paper's contribution and publishability in its current form.

Overview issues:

- The variational lower bound derivation is conceptually flawed: In Section 3.1, the paper writes the expected log-likelihood as a KL divergence plus a variational lower bound (Eq. 4). However, the derivation is circular: the KL divergence is between the true noisy posterior p(Ỹ|X) and the variational approximation q(Ỹ|X), but the true noisy posterior is exactly what the paper aims to model. The variational bound is supposed to lower-bound the marginal likelihood, not the expected log-likelihood under the true model. The paper never defines a proper generative model or a variational distribution over latent variables; it simply replaces the true transition matrix with a simpler function. This is not variational inference in any standard sense. The claim that maximizing L(θ,φ) approximates the true likelihood is unsupported. The paper needs to either provide a correct derivation or drop the variational language entirely and present ICE as a heuristic approximation.

- The single-parameter approximation lacks theoretical justification: Section 2.3 argues that when the true label is nearly deterministic, most columns of the transition matrix have limited influence on the noisy posterior, motivating a single-parameter approximation. This reasoning is incomplete. First, the argument assumes the clean posterior p(Y|X) is known and near-deterministic, but in practice the classifier is being learned simultaneously and may be far from deterministic, especially early in training. Second, even if the clean posterior is peaked, the transition matrix columns for the low-probability classes can still significantly affect the noisy posterior if those columns have large off-diagonal entries. The paper provides no analysis of the approximation error or conditions under which the single-parameter model is faithful. A rigorous bound or a simulation study demonstrating the approximation's fidelity under various noise regimes is needed.

- The instance embedding approach is not properly compared to alternatives: Section 3.3 motivates instance embedding by arguing that the confidence function g may be non-smooth and that generalization is unnecessary. However, the paper does not compare instance embedding against a neural network approximation for g in the main experiments (Table 1 only shows ICE results). Figure 3 shows a toy example but no quantitative comparison. This is a critical omission: if a simple neural network head for confidence estimation works as well, the instance embedding's lack of generalization is a major disadvantage. The paper should include an ablation study comparing ICE against a version where g is a small neural network, on the same benchmarks, to justify the instance embedding design choice.

- The experimental evaluation lacks real-world noisy datasets: The paper evaluates only on datasets with synthetic instance-dependent noise at a fixed 52% noise rate. Real-world noisy datasets like Clothing1M and WebVision are standard in the label noise literature and are mentioned in the paper's own evaluation standards. Without results on such datasets, it is unclear whether ICE's instance embedding approach works in practice, where noise patterns may differ from the synthetic generation process. The paper should include experiments on at least one real-world noisy dataset to demonstrate practical relevance.

- No ablation studies on key components: The paper claims ICE has two key components: the variational approximation (with two variants: linear interpolation and power transformation) and the instance embedding. However, there are no ablation studies isolating the contribution of each component. For example, does the power transformation consistently outperform linear interpolation? Does instance embedding help more than a learned neural network confidence estimator? The paper also does not study sensitivity to the embedding dimension (though it uses 1D) or to the initialization of the confidence parameters. These ablations are standard for this type of paper and are necessary to support the design choices.

- The claim about detecting mislabeled instances is not rigorously validated: Section 5.1 and 5.2 present qualitative examples of low-confidence instances and claim the method can detect mislabeled or ambiguous examples. However, the paper does not provide quantitative metrics for detection performance (e.g., precision/recall, AUC) on the synthetic datasets where ground truth is known. For the real datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, CoLA), the paper merely shows cherry-picked examples. Without systematic evaluation, the claim that ICE 'can effectively detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances' (abstract) is not supported. The paper should report detection accuracy on the synthetic noise datasets and, for real datasets, provide human evaluation or comparison with existing data-cleaning methods.

- Missing statistical significance and variance reporting: Table 1 reports means and standard deviations over 10 trials, which is good. However, the paper does not report whether the improvements over baselines are statistically significant (beyond a t-test mentioned in the caption but not applied consistently). For many datasets, the standard deviations overlap between ICE and the best baseline (e.g., on CIFAR-10, ICE-POW 82.37 vs PLC 82.51, with stds 0.29 and 0.34). The paper should report paired significance tests or confidence intervals to support claims of improvement. Additionally, the GLUE results (Table 2) report only a single run with no variance, making it impossible to assess reliability.

- No real-world noisy dataset evaluation: The paper evaluates only on synthetic instance-dependent noise at a fixed 52% noise rate. Real-world noisy datasets like Clothing1M and WebVision are standard in the label noise literature and are mentioned in the paper's own evaluation standards. Without results on such datasets, it is unclear whether ICE's instance embedding approach works in practice, where noise patterns may differ from the synthetic generation process. The paper should include experiments on at least one real-world noisy dataset to demonstrate practical relevance.

- Missing ablation study comparing instance embedding to neural network confidence estimator: Section 3.3 motivates instance embedding by arguing that the confidence function g may be non-smooth and that generalization is unnecessary. However, the paper does not compare instance embedding against a neural network approximation for g in the main experiments. Figure 3 shows a toy example but no quantitative comparison. This is a critical omission: if a simple neural network head for confidence estimation works as well, the instance embedding's lack of generalization is a major disadvantage. The paper should include an ablation study comparing ICE against a version where g is a small neural network, on the same benchmarks, to justify the instance embedding design choice.

- No quantitative evaluation of mislabeled instance detection: The paper claims ICE can effectively detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances, but provides only qualitative examples. For the synthetic datasets where ground truth is known, the paper should report detection metrics such as AUC or precision/recall for identifying flipped labels. For real datasets, the paper should provide human evaluation or comparison with existing data-cleaning methods. Without systematic evaluation, the claim that ICE 'can effectively detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances' (abstract) is not supported.

- No discussion of how to estimate or tune the confidence embedding in practice: The paper derives a theoretical quantity (the scalar confidence parameter C) but provides no practical guidance on how to estimate it. For instance, how should the confidence parameters be initialized? What regularization, if any, is applied to prevent them from collapsing to 0 or 1? How does the method handle the curse of dimensionality when the number of training instances is large? A paper that claims practical relevance but provides no practical guidance has a notable gap.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper addresses an important problem and proposes a simple, appealing method, but the theoretical foundation is unsound, the experimental evaluation is incomplete, and key claims are unsubstantiated. A revision must fix the variational derivation, add real-world experiments and ablations, and provide rigorous validation of the detection claims.

Revision targets:

- Correct the variational lower bound derivation in Section 3.1. Either provide a proper generative model with latent variables and a valid ELBO, or reframe ICE as a heuristic approximation without variational claims.

- Add experiments on at least one real-world noisy dataset (e.g., Clothing1M, WebVision) to demonstrate practical relevance.

- Include ablation studies comparing instance embedding against a neural network confidence estimator, and comparing the two transformation functions (linear interpolation vs. power transformation).

- Provide quantitative evaluation of mislabeled instance detection on synthetic noise datasets (precision/recall or AUC) and systematic evaluation on real datasets.

- Report statistical significance (paired tests or confidence intervals) for all main results, and include variance for the GLUE experiments.

comments.title: Expected log-likelihood objective couples target and approximation parameters
comments.quote:
Then, let us consider the *expected log-likelihood* as the learning objective, which can be rewritten as

$\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{\widetilde{Y}\sim p(\widetilde{Y}|X)}[\log p(\widetilde{Y}|X)]=D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p_{\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)\parallel q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X))+\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;X),$ (4)

where $D_{\mathrm{KL}}$ denotes the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, and the second term is

$\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;X)\coloneqq\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{\widetilde{Y}\sim p(\widetilde{Y}|X)}[\log q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)].$ (5)

Since the KL-divergence is always non-negative, this term gives a *variational lower bound* of the expected log-likelihood. Then, we have the following learning objective to maximize:

$L(\theta,\phi)\coloneqq\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{X\sim p(X)}[\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;X)]=\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{X,\widetilde{Y}\sim p(X,\widetilde{Y})}[\log q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)].$ (6)
comments.explanation: Both p_φ(Ỹ|X) and q_{θ,φ}(Ỹ|X) depend on φ (the clean posterior parameters). This coupling is non-standard: in variational inference the target is fixed, but here optimizing φ changes both the target and the approximation simultaneously. The paper does not discuss whether this can lead to degenerate solutions (e.g., φ collapsing both distributions to trivial values) or what prevents it. The authors should add a discussion of the optimization landscape, potential degeneracies, and how the specific parameterization of q in Section 3.2 avoids them.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about limited influence of transition matrix columns
comments.quote: Note that $p_{\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|x)$ can be seen as a linear combination of columns of $\bm{T}(x)$ weighted by $p_{\phi}(Y|x)$; If the maximum value of $p_{\phi}(Y|x)$ is close to $1$, i.e., the label of the instance $x$ is almost deterministic, the estimation of $K-1$ columns of $\bm{T}(x)$ has only limited influence on the estimated noisy class-posterior $\widehat{p}(\widetilde{Y}|x)$. This suggests the possibility of using a relatively simple model to approximate $p(\widetilde{Y}|X)$ in real-world applications.
comments.explanation: The argument that columns of T(x) for low-probability classes have limited influence is incomplete. The noisy posterior is a weighted sum of columns. If p_φ(Y|x) is peaked at class i, column i dominates. But the other columns can still significantly affect the noisy posterior if they have large off-diagonal entries (e.g., T_{j,i}(x) large for j ≠ i). The paper provides no analysis of the approximation error or conditions under which the single-parameter model is faithful. A rigorous bound or simulation study is needed.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about generalization of T(x) being dispensable
comments.quote: Another issue is that existing methods still introduce some level of smoothness w.r.t. $x$ into $\bm{T}(x)$ *(Goldberger and Ben-Reuven, 2017; Xiao et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2020)*. In real-world problems, however, we can only access a finite sample of $(X,\widetilde{Y})$-pairs that are possibly annotated by non-experts or web crawlers *(Fergus et al., 2005)*. Thus, the label noise could be aleatory and $\bm{T}(x)$ could vary significantly for two adjacent instances. Also, the classifier $p_{\phi}(Y|X)$ is desired but the generalization of $\bm{T}(x)$ to unseen examples is usually dispensable. This inspires us to use *instance embedding* instead of neural network approximation, which is discussed in Section 3.3 and demonstrated in Fig. 3.
comments.explanation: The claim that generalization of T(x) is 'usually dispensable' is unsupported and contradicts standard practice. The training procedure relies on the estimated noisy posterior for each training instance. Without generalization of T(x), the method cannot handle new instances during training (e.g., in mini-batch SGD where instances are sampled without replacement). The paper should clarify how the instance embedding is used at test time or justify why generalization is not needed. At test time, the classifier f is used directly without the instance embedding, or the noise transition matrix is fixed to the average over training instances. This should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Missing justification for factorization in equation (2)
comments.quote:
In IDN, we assume that the joint distribution of  $X$ ,  $Y$ , and  $\widetilde{Y}$  can be factorized as follows:

$$
p (X, Y, \widetilde {Y}) = p (\widetilde {Y} | Y, X) p _ {\phi} (Y | X) p (X). \tag {2}
$$

That is, the noisy label  $\widetilde{Y}$  depends on both the instance  $X$  and the true label  $Y$ .
comments.explanation: The factorization in equation (2) is presented as the definition of IDN, but it is actually a generic factorization that holds for any joint distribution over (X, Y, Ỹ) by the chain rule of probability. The key assumption that distinguishes IDN from other noise models (e.g., class-conditional noise) is that p(Ỹ|Y, X) depends on X, not just Y. The text should explicitly state this assumption rather than presenting the factorization itself as the defining property.

comments.title: Unclear justification for argmax-preserving requirement
comments.quote: $\arg \max(\pmb{p}) = \arg \max(\pmb{q})$ , i.e.,  $\pmb{q} = h(\pmb{p}; C)$  should be an argmax-preserving function so that the top-1 index of the probability vector does not change. This is because  $h$  should only affect the confidence of the prediction, not the final decision. Otherwise, if  $h$  is too flexible and is able to map a confident prediction to a different confident prediction, then the output of  $f$  could be arbitrary, and consequently, no information of the true label can be learned from the noisy label supervision.
comments.explanation: The argument that an argmax-preserving transformation is necessary to prevent the classifier from learning arbitrary outputs is not logically justified. The noisy label Ỹ is generated from the true label Y via the transition matrix T(x), which can change the argmax. The variational distribution q(Ỹ|X) is meant to approximate p(Ỹ|X), which may have a different argmax than p(Y|X). Requiring h to preserve the argmax of p(Y|X) imposes a constraint that may not hold for the true noisy posterior, potentially introducing bias. The paper should justify why this constraint is necessary for learning, or relax it.

comments.title: Power transformation may violate argmax-preserving property at C=0
comments.explanation: The power transformation in Eq. (9) is claimed to satisfy the argmax-preserving condition. However, this is only true if C > 0. If C = 0, then p_i^0 = 1 for all i, so q becomes uniform, and the argmax is not preserved (it becomes undefined or all indices). The paper requires h(p; 0) = u, which is consistent, but the argmax-preserving condition is stated as a general requirement for h. The paper should explicitly note that the argmax-preserving condition holds for C > 0, and that C = 0 is a special case where the output is uniform and the argmax is not meaningful.

comments.title: Instance embedding vs. neural network comparison lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: It is also possible to use a neural network to approximate this function. However, because we usually only have a limited number of training examples and  $g$  could be non-smooth w.r.t. its input  $x$ ,  $g$  may not be well approximated by a neural network with similar complexity to the classifier  $f$ , which is illustrated in an example in Fig. 3. Further,  $g$  may be rarely needed after training so its generalization ability is not required
comments.explanation: The argument against using a neural network for g relies on a single illustrative example (Fig. 3) and the claim of non-smoothness. No quantitative comparison or ablation is provided to demonstrate that a neural network approximation would actually perform worse. The authors should either include an ablation study comparing ICE with instance embedding against a version where g is a small neural network head, or soften the claim to acknowledge that both approaches are possible and instance embedding is chosen for simplicity.

comments.title: Computational cost comparison is misleading
comments.quote: This seems to be a high additional computational cost when the dataset size is large, but it is often acceptable, because (i) in modern deep learning, it is common to use over-parameterized models (Nakkiran et al., 2019), and the number of instances is usually not as large as the number of parameters of the classifier  $f$  (e.g., CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009):  $5 \times 10^{4}$ , ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016):  $\sim 1 \times 10^{7}$ ); and (ii) the gradient of the instance embedding is sparse and only a small subset of parameters needs to be updated at
comments.explanation: The parameter count comparison (50,000 vs. 10 million) is misleading because the instance embedding parameters are not interchangeable with classifier parameters. The embedding adds 50,000 parameters that grow linearly with dataset size, and for large datasets like ImageNet (1.2 million images), this becomes non-negligible. The claim about gradient sparsity does not address memory costs. The paper should provide a more honest assessment of scalability limitations. For example: 'While the number of instance embedding parameters is typically smaller than the classifier parameters (e.g., CIFAR-10: 50,000 vs. 10 million), for very large datasets (e.g., ImageNet: 1.2 million), the embedding size becomes non-negligible. Memory costs of storing all embeddings should also be considered.'

comments.title: Missing justification for non-smoothness claim
comments.quote: However, because we usually only have a limited number of training examples and  $g$  could be non-smooth w.r.t. its input  $x$ ,  $g$  may not be well approximated by a neural network with similar complexity to the classifier  $f$ , which is illustrated in an example in Fig. 3.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that g could be non-smooth w.r.t. x, but provides no formal definition or justification for this non-smoothness. The example in Fig. 3 is referenced but not described in the text. Without a clear explanation of why g is expected to be non-smooth (e.g., due to label noise patterns or decision boundaries), the argument against using a neural network is incomplete. The authors should either provide a theoretical justification for the non-smoothness of g or cite empirical evidence.

comments.title: Missing justification for expectation over X
comments.quote:
Then, we have the following learning objective to maximize:

$L(\theta,\phi)\coloneqq\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{X\sim p(X)}[\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;X)]=\mathop{\mathbb{E}}_{X,\widetilde{Y}\sim p(X,\widetilde{Y})}[\log q_{\theta,\phi}(\widetilde{Y}|X)].$ (6)

In practice, the expectation can be approximated using the empirical distribution based on a finite i.i.d. sample of $(X,\widetilde{Y})$-pairs.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the expectation over X in Eq. (6) is taken with respect to p(X), the marginal distribution of clean inputs. However, the joint distribution p(X,Ỹ) in the second equality is the distribution of clean inputs and noisy labels. The paper does not specify how p(X) relates to the data generating process. In standard noisy-label settings, the marginal distribution of X is the same for clean and noisy data (the noise process only affects labels). But the paper's notation is ambiguous: p(X) could be the marginal of clean inputs, while the empirical sample is of (X,Ỹ) pairs from the noisy process. The authors should clarify that p(X) is the marginal of X under the noisy joint distribution, or explicitly state that the noise process does not change the marginal of X.

comments.title: Linear interpolation argmax-preserving property needs clarification at C=0
comments.explanation: The linear interpolation in Eq. (8) is claimed to satisfy the argmax-preserving condition. In general, since u is uniform, adding (1-C)u adds the same amount to each component, so the ordering of components is preserved. Thus the argmax is preserved for all C in [0,1], including C=0 where q = u (all components equal, argmax is all indices). The paper should note that at C=0, the argmax is not unique, but the condition still holds in the sense that no component is strictly larger than another.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a method called Instance-Confidence Embedding (ICE) to approximate instance-dependent noise (IDN) by using a scalar confidence parameter and per-instance embeddings. While the empirical results show competitive performance, the theoretical justification for the approximation is weak, and the method introduces significant scalability concerns for large datasets. Furthermore, the claim that the method robustly detects mislabeled instances relies on ambiguous examples that could simply be difficult samples.

This work offers a practical approach to handling label noise by decoupling the noise model from the classifier. The experiments demonstrate that the method outperforms standard baselines and provides a mechanism for identifying potentially noisy data. However, the paper fails to provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for why a single scalar parameter suffices to approximate the complex matrix-valued transition functions inherent in the IDN model. Additionally, the reliance on per-instance parameters raises scalability questions that are not adequately addressed.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Gap Between IDN Model and Approximation: The paper defines the general IDN model where the transition matrix $T(x)$ can map a confident prediction $ oldsymbol{p}$ to any noisy distribution $ oldsymbol{q}$. However, the proposed approximation $q_{	heta,	heta}(	ilde{Y}|X)$ in Eq. (8) and (9) imposes a strict constraint: $	ext{argmax}( oldsymbol{p}) = 	ext{argmax}( oldsymbol{q})$. This means the method cannot handle cases where the noise flips the top prediction to a different class (e.g., a cat image labeled as a dog). The paper claims this restriction is acceptable because confident predictions are robust, but this is an empirical observation, not a theoretical guarantee. The authors need to clarify whether their method is actually a subset of IDN (specifically handling 'confidence degradation' or 'random guessing' noise) or if it is fundamentally limited in capturing label-flipping noise.

- Risk of Memorization via Per-Instance Parameters: The method assigns a trainable scalar parameter $C$ to every training instance via the instance embedding. The learning objective is simply the cross-entropy loss. This setup creates a risk that the model will learn to assign $C 	o 0$ for instances that are difficult to classify or noisy, effectively ignoring them from the loss calculation. While the paper argues that the gradient for these parameters is sparse, this does not prevent the model from overfitting by simply memorizing the difficulty of each specific example rather than learning a generalizable representation of the data distribution. The authors should provide an analysis or theoretical bound showing that the model learns meaningful confidence scores rather than just 'hardness' scores that correlate with the loss value.

- Scalability of Instance Embedding: The paper justifies the use of instance embedding by stating that the number of instances $N$ is usually smaller than the number of parameters in the classifier $f$. While this may hold for small datasets like CIFAR-10, it fails for large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet, large text corpora) where $N$ can reach millions. Storing $N$ scalars for the instance embedding results in significant memory overhead ($O(N)$) that is not negligible in modern deep learning pipelines. The paper should discuss the memory footprint and scalability of the method to large-scale datasets, or provide a justification for why the method is intended for small-to-medium datasets only.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to label smoothing
comments.quote:
el smoothing.

Note that Eq. (8) is similar to the *label smoothing* (LS) technique *(Szegedy et al., 2016; Pereyra et al., 2017; Lukasik et al., 2020)*, where the empirical distribution is linearly interpolated with a uniform distribution with a fixed mixing parameter. It is also related to the soft/hard *bootstrapping loss* *(Reed et al., 2015)*, where the observed label is mixed with the predicted probability/predicted label. In contrast, in our method, it is the prediction $\bm{p}$ that is “smoothed”,
comments.explanation: The comparison to label smoothing is confusing. Standard label smoothing modifies the *target* distribution (the label), whereas Eq. (8) modifies the *prediction* $p$. The text acknowledges this distinction but starts by claiming similarity, which is misleading. Clarify that while the interpolation form is similar, the target of the operation differs.

comments.title: Section scope mismatch (Image vs NLP)
comments.quote: Table 2: Performance (%) on the GLUE benchmark for natural language understanding. We reported Matthews correlation coefficient on CoLA, F1 score/accuracy on MRPC and QQP, and accuracy otherwise. MNLI-(m/mm) denotes MultiNLI matched/mismatched, respectively.
comments.explanation: Section 5.1 is titled 'IMAGE CLASSIFICATION', yet the text immediately following Figure 6 reports results on the GLUE benchmark (CoLA, SST2, etc.). This content belongs in a separate NLP section. Please restructure to avoid mixing image and text classification results in the same section.

comments.title: Ambiguity between synthetic IDN and intrinsic noise
comments.quote: We regarded the original labels as clean labels, although as will be shown in the next experiment, label errors already exist in the original datasets to some extent.
comments.explanation: The paper mixes two experimental contexts: (1) synthetic IDN for performance comparison, and (2) intrinsic noise detection on original datasets. The sentence 'We regarded the original labels as clean labels' is ambiguous because it refers to the synthetic setup but uses the term 'original datasets' which implies the intrinsic noise experiment. Distinguish these clearly.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to the proposed method
comments.quote: We compared the following ten methods: (1) (CCE) categorical cross-entropy loss; (2) (Bootstrapping) (hard) bootstrapping loss (Reed et al., 2015) that regularizes the output with the predicted label; (3) (Adaptation) noise adaptation layer (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017) that estimates a full  $K \times K$  transition matrix for each instance; (4) (Forward) forward correction (Patrini et al., 2017) that estimates a transition matrix for all instances; (5) (Dual-T) dual-T estimator (Yao et al., 2020) that uses the normalized confusion matrix to correct the transition matrix; (6) (DAC) deep abstaining classifier (Thulasidasan et al., 2019) that uses abstention for robust learning; (7) (GCE) generalized cross-entropy loss (Zhang & Sabuncu, 2018) as a robust loss; (8) (PLC) progressive label correction (Zhang et al., 2021a); (9) (ICE-LIN) instance-confidence embedding with the linear interpolation (Eq. (8)); and (10) (ICE-POW) the one with the power transformation (Eq.
comments.explanation: The list enumerates ICE-LIN and ICE-POW as methods to compare, but does not explicitly designate one as the primary contribution. While implied, it is better to state 'We propose ICE-LIN (and ICE-POW as a variant)' to avoid ambiguity.
